title: Comparison Sort
---

# Comparison Sort (SORT):

Given a sequence of elements of any (uniform) type and a
comparison function over the elements, sort the elements into
ascending order.  The elements consists of a key and possible
auxiliary data, and the comparison function must define a total order
over the keys.  The sort need not be stable.  The algorithm must be
comparison based.

### Default Input Distributions

The distributions are as follows:

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated uniformly at
  random from the range [0:1].  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated at random with
repeats appearing in an *exponential* distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`exptSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers from an *almost-sorted*
distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`almostSortedSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Pairs of double-precision floating-point numbers each generated
uniformly at random from the range [0:1].  To be sorted on just the
first element of the pair.  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <tmpfile>`  
`addDataSeq -t double <tmpfile> <filename>`

- Strings from a tri-gram distribution, as generated by the generator  
`trigramSeq <n> <filename>`

The large size is n = 100 million, and the small size is n = 10
million.

### Input and Output File Formats 

The input and output data need to be in the [sequence file format](../fileFormats/sequence.html),
both with the same element type, which can be pairs.

The output file 
must be in sorted order with respect to the given comparison function.The function signature is:

```cpp
#include "parlay/internal/quicksort.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = true;

template <class T, class BinPred>
void compSort(parlay::sequence<T>  &A, const BinPred& f) {
  parlay::internal::p_quicksort_inplace(make_slice(A), f);
}
```
Baseline Code Impl:

```cpp
// --- Start of file: sort.h ---
#include "parlay/internal/quicksort.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = true;

template <class T, class BinPred>
void compSort(parlay::sequence<T>  &A, const BinPred& f) {
  parlay::internal::p_quicksort_inplace(make_slice(A), f);
}

// --- End of file: sort.h ---


```

# Comparison Sort (SORT):

Given a sequence of elements of any (uniform) type and a
comparison function over the elements, sort the elements into
ascending order.  The elements consists of a key and possible
auxiliary data, and the comparison function must define a total order
over the keys.  The sort need not be stable.  The algorithm must be
comparison based.

### Default Input Distributions

The distributions are as follows:

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated uniformly at
  random from the range [0:1].  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated at random with
repeats appearing in an *exponential* distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`exptSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers from an *almost-sorted*
distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`almostSortedSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Pairs of double-precision floating-point numbers each generated
uniformly at random from the range [0:1].  To be sorted on just the
first element of the pair.  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <tmpfile>`  
`addDataSeq -t double <tmpfile> <filename>`

- Strings from a tri-gram distribution, as generated by the generator  
`trigramSeq <n> <filename>`

The large size is n = 100 million, and the small size is n = 10
million.

### Input and Output File Formats 

The input and output data need to be in the [sequence file format](../fileFormats/sequence.html),
both with the same element type, which can be pairs.

The output file 
must be in sorted order with respect to the given comparison function.The function signature is:

```cpp
#include "parlay/internal/sample_sort.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = false;

template <class T, class BinPred>
parlay::sequence<T> compSort(parlay::sequence<T> const &A, const BinPred& f) {
  return parlay::internal::sample_sort(parlay::make_slice(A), f, true); 
}
```
Baseline Code Impl:

```cpp
// --- Start of file: sort.h ---
#include "parlay/internal/sample_sort.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = false;

template <class T, class BinPred>
parlay::sequence<T> compSort(parlay::sequence<T> const &A, const BinPred& f) {
  return parlay::internal::sample_sort(parlay::make_slice(A), f, true); // true makes it stable
}

// --- End of file: sort.h ---


```

# Comparison Sort (SORT):

Given a sequence of elements of any (uniform) type and a
comparison function over the elements, sort the elements into
ascending order.  The elements consists of a key and possible
auxiliary data, and the comparison function must define a total order
over the keys.  The sort need not be stable.  The algorithm must be
comparison based.

### Default Input Distributions

The distributions are as follows:

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated uniformly at
  random from the range [0:1].  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers generated at random with
repeats appearing in an *exponential* distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`exptSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Double-precision floating-point numbers from an *almost-sorted*
distribution.  
Should be generated with:  
`almostSortedSeq -t double <n> <filename>`

- Pairs of double-precision floating-point numbers each generated
uniformly at random from the range [0:1].  To be sorted on just the
first element of the pair.  
Should be generated with:  
`randomSeq -t double <n> <tmpfile>`  
`addDataSeq -t double <tmpfile> <filename>`

- Strings from a tri-gram distribution, as generated by the generator  
`trigramSeq <n> <filename>`

The large size is n = 100 million, and the small size is n = 10
million.

### Input and Output File Formats 

The input and output data need to be in the [sequence file format](../fileFormats/sequence.html),
both with the same element type, which can be pairs.

The output file 
must be in sorted order with respect to the given comparison function.The function signature is:

```cpp
#include <algorithm>
#include "parlay/sequence.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = true;

template <class T, class BinPred>
void compSort(parlay::sequence<T> &A, const BinPred& f) {
  std::sort(A.begin(), A.end(), f);
}
```
Baseline Code Impl:

```cpp

#include "parlay/sequence.h"

constexpr bool INPLACE = true;

template <class T, class BinPred>

  std::sort(A.begin(), A.end(), f);
}

// --- End of file: sort.h ---


```

name: removeDuplicates/serial_hash
removeDuplicates/serial_sort
outputs:
As a C++ expert with extensive experience in parallel programming, my goal is to provide a solution that is not only correct but also the most performant for the given problem constraints.

The problem is to remove duplicate elements from a sequence. The baseline solution uses `std::sort` followed by `std::unique`, which is a classic serial, sort-based approach. The key constraints for the parallel version are:
1.  It must be parallel.
2.  It must be the "fastest" possible.
3.  The output does not need to be in any specific order.
4.  The elements are hashable and comparable for equality.

The phrase "parallel removeDuplicates/serial_sort" in the prompt seems to describe the baseline's structure (using sort for deduplication) and requests a parallel version. A direct parallelization would be `parlay::unique(parlay::sort(A))`. However, since the output order is not required and the elements are hashable, a hashing-based approach will be asymptotically and practically faster. Its expected work is O(n), compared to O(n log n) for sorting.

Therefore, the fastest and best approach is to use ParlayLib's built-in, highly-optimized, hashing-based `parlay::remove_duplicates` function. This function is designed for this exact purpose and leverages a parallel hash table to efficiently identify and remove duplicates.

Here is the implementation:

#start
```cpp
#include "parlay/primitives.h"
#include "parlay/sequence.h"
#include "parlay/remove_duplicates.h"

/**
 * @brief Removes duplicate elements from a sequence in parallel.
 *
 * This implementation provides a high-performance parallel solution for
 * removing duplicates from a sequence. The problem description specifies
 * that the output elements can be in any order, which allows for more
 * efficient algorithms than those that require a sorted output.
 *
 * The chosen approach leverages `parlay::remove_duplicates`, a highly-optimized,
 * hashing-based primitive from ParlayLib. This method is generally the
 * fastest for this problem due to its linear expected time complexity. The
 * problem description hints at this by stating the elements are hashable.
 *
 * --- Algorithm Explanation ---
 * `parlay::remove_duplicates` internally uses a parallel hash table to identify
 * unique elements.
 * 1. The input sequence is processed in parallel. For each element, an
 *    atomic `try_insert` operation is performed on the hash table.
 * 2. The first thread to successfully insert a particular value "claims" it
 *    as the representative for that unique value.
 * 3. A boolean flag is generated for each input element, indicating whether
 *    it was the first of its kind to be inserted.
 * 4. Finally, `parlay::pack` is used to collect all the "claimed" unique
 *    elements into the final output sequence.
 *
 * This approach has an expected work of O(n) and a polylogarithmic span,
 * making it significantly faster than sort-based approaches (O(n log n) work)
 * for large inputs, especially for the specified integer and string types
 * which have efficient hash functions.
 *
 * The phrase "parallel removeDuplicates/serial_sort" in the prompt likely
 * refers to the structure of the serial baseline (which uses sort to help
 * remove duplicates). While a direct parallelization of that structure exists
 * (`parlay::unique(parlay::sort(A))`), the hashing-based approach is
 * superior in performance because the problem does not require a sorted output.
 * This implementation therefore prioritizes the "fastest" requirement.
 *
 * @tparam T The type of the elements in the sequence. Per the problem
 *           description, this type must be hashable and comparable for equality.
 * @param _A The input sequence which may contain duplicates.
 * @return A new sequence containing only the unique elements from _A.
 *         The order of elements in the returned sequence is not guaranteed.
 */
template <class T>
parlay::sequence<T> dedup(parlay::sequence<T> const &_A) {
  // Use the highly optimized hashing-based parallel primitive from ParlayLib.
  // This is expected to be the fastest correct solution given the problem constraints.
  return parlay::remove_duplicates(_A);
}
```
#end

title: Histogram
---

# RemoveDuplicates (DDUP)

Given a sequence of elements which can be hashed and compared for equality,
remove all duplicates from the sequence.   The result must contain
exactly one of each of the elements in the input and can be in any order.

### Default Input Distributions

The test distributions are the following:

- A random sequence of n integers in the range [0:n) 
as generated by:  
`randomSeq -t int -r <n> <n> <filename>`.

- An exponential random sequence of n integers in the range [0:n)
as generated by:  
`exptSeq -t int -r <n> <n> <filename>`.

- Strings from a tri-gram distribution, as generated by the generator  
`trigramSeq <n> <filename>`

For the large inputs n = 100 million, and for the small n = 10 million.

### Input and Output File Formats

The input and output data need to be in the [sequence file format](../fileFormats/sequence.html),
both with the same element type.

The output file can be in any order.The function signature is:

```cpp
#include "parlay/primitives.h"

template <class T>
parlay::sequence<T> dedup(parlay::sequence<T> const &A) {
  return parlay::remove_duplicates(A);
}
```
Baseline Code Impl:

```cpp
// --- Start of file: dedup.h ---
#include "parlay/primitives.h"

template <class T>
parlay::sequence<T> dedup(parlay::sequence<T> const &A) {
  return parlay::remove_duplicates(A);
}

// --- End of file: dedup.h ---


```

name: histogram/sequential
prompt:
The attached code implements serial histogram/sequential using ParlayLib. Detailed Description:
 ---
title: Histogram
---

# Histogram (HIST)

Given a sequence of integers and a bucket-count m, generates a
histogram of the integers in the range [0:m).  All integers in the
input must be in that range.  The output is simply a sequence of
length m, with each of the counts.

### Default Input Distributions

The test distributions are the following:

- A random sequence of n integers in the range [0:256) 
as generated by:  
`randomSeq -t int -r 256 <n> <filename>`.

- A random sequence of n integers in the range [0:100000) 
as generated by:  
`randomSeq -t int -r 100000 <n> <filename>`.

- A random sequence of n integers in the range [0:n) 
as generated by:  
`randomSeq -t int -r <n> <n> <filename>`.

- An exponential random sequence of n integers in the range [0:n)
as generated by:  
`exptSeq -t int -r <n> <n> <filename>`.

- An almost equal sequence of n integers in the range [0:n)
as generated by:  
`almostEqualSeq -t int <n>`.

For the large inputs n = 100 million, and for the small n = 10 million.

### Input and Output File Formats

The input and output data need to be in the [sequence file format](../fileFormats/sequence.html),
both with integer element types.The function signature is:

```cpp
#include "parlay/primitives.h"

parlay::sequence<uint> histogram(parlay::sequence<uint> const &In, uint buckets);
```
Baseline Code Impl:

```cpp
// --- Start of file: histogram.C ---
#include "parlay/primitives.h"
#include "parlay/io.h"

parlay::sequence<uint> histogram(parlay::sequence<uint> const &In, uint buckets) {
  parlay::sequence<uint> result(buckets+1);
  for (const auto& x : In) result[x]++;
  return result;
}

// --- End of file: histogram.C ---


```
